• Option A: Moroteaux-Lamy disease: Deficiency of N-Acetyl-β D Galactosamine- 4- Sulfatase , C/F- Skeletal deformity, corneal opacity, No MR and Dermatan Sulfate in urine.
• Option B: Hunter’s disease: Deficiency of iduronate sulfatase, C/F- MR, Skeletal deformity, deafness, no corneal clouding with Dermatan Sulfate (DS) and Heparan Sulfate in urine.
• Option D: Scheie’s diseases: Deficiency of L-Iduronidase , C/F- No MR with mild skeletal changes, corneal opacity and DS in urine.
• Educational Objective:
• Hurler’s disease (Mucopolysaccharidosis type I), a lysosomal storage disorder caused by a deficiency of the enzyme alpha-L-iduronidase. This deficiency leads to the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans, which results in the observed clinical features such as organomegaly (leading to a protuberant abdomen), umbilical hernia , coarse facies, corneal clouding , intellectual disability , and growth retardation.
• Harper 30/e:  p 638-639

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 25-year-old patient presents with a genetic anomaly affecting the activity of disaccharidases produced by the intestinal epithelial cells. These enzymes are responsible for the digestion of various disaccharides into monosaccharides, which can then be absorbed into the bloodstream. After consuming a meal consisting of milk and oatmeal, both of which contain disaccharides, which of the following would you expect to find in higher levels in this patient compared to a healthy individual?
options: Galactose and fructose in the blood.
Maltose, sucrose, and lactose in the stool

explanation.short: Ans. C) Maltose, sucrose, and lactose in the stool
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Galactose and fructose in the blood would be lower , not higher, because these monosaccharides result from the digestion of lactose and sucrose, respectively, which is impaired in this patient.
• Option B: Starch in the stool would not necessarily be higher because starch digestion begins with salivary and pancreatic amylase , not disaccharidases.
• Option D: Glycogen in the muscles would be unaffected directly by disaccharidase activity as it is dependent on the uptake of glucose from the blood, which would be reduced due to the malabsorption of its precursor disaccharides.
• Educational Objective:
• Starch: digested to small oligosaccharides and maltose but produce lower quantity of glucose due to deficiency of the brush border disaccharidases, which include sucrase and lactase. So, Sucrose and lactose will not be cleaved and will accumulate.
• Harper 30/e:  p 538, 599

subject: Biochemistry
question: A patient with a long-standing history of type I diabetes mellitus has been diligently adhering to insulin therapy, yet consistently records fasting and postprandial blood glucose levels that exceed the normal range. To assist in better glucose regulation, a dietician suggests an increased intake of dietary fiber. Among the Option listed below, which type of dietary fiber is most beneficial for managing blood glucose levels in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Cellulose : These are polymers of glucose. It retains water in feces; promotes peristalsis and increases bowel action. It is the starting material for production of fibres, celluloids, nitrocellulose, and plastics.
• Option B: Hemicellulose : These include pentoses, hexoses and uronic acid, which retains water in feces and increases bile acid excretion.
• Option C: Lignin : They are aromatic alcohols with hypocholesterolemic, antioxidant action and also increases bile acid excretion.
• Educational Objective:
• Dietary fibers are classified as soluble (pectin, gums, mucilages) or insoluble (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin). Soluble fibers like pectin form viscous gels that slow digestion and absorption of carbohydrates, making them particularly beneficial for blood glucose management in diabetes. Pectin is found in fruits (especially apples, citrus fruits), berries, and some vegetables. Insoluble fibers primarily improve bowel health and regularity without significantly affecting blood glucose levels directly.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A patient with a history of acute glomerulonephritis, who is currently under treatment, attends a routine follow-up appointment. The focus is on the integrity and function of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) in relation to kidney function. Which mucopolysaccharide present in the GBM is primarily responsible for maintaining the charge-based filtration function of the kidney?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Chondroitin sulphate: present in the ground substance of connective tissues which is distributed in cartilage, bone, tendons, cornea and skin.
• Option C: Hyaluronic acid: present in connective tissues , tendons , synovial fluid and vitreous humor and acts as a lubricant and shock absorber in joint cavities.
• Option D: Keratan sulphate: only GAG, which does not contain uronic acid and found in cornea and tendons , thereby maintaining corneal transparency.
• Educational Objective:
• Heparan sulphate is a key component of the glomerular basement membrane and is vital for its normal function . It provides the GBM with a negative charge , which is essential for the selective filtration of molecules based on size and charge, preventing the passage of negatively charged proteins such as albumin into the urine.
• Harper 30/e:  p 638, Lippincott 7/e : P 159

subject: Biochemistry
question: A middle-aged man presents with a diagnosis of mucopolysaccharidosis, characterized by the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs). One GAG that has accumulated is known to play a critical role in the charge selectiveness of the renal glomerular membrane. Which of the following GAGs is responsible for maintaining the charge selectiveness of the renal glomerular membrane?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B: Dermatan sulphate : found in skin, blood vessels and heart valves and determines the elasticity of heart valve and skin.
• Option C: Hyaluronic acid : present in connective tissues, tendons, synovial fluid and vitreous humor and acts as a lubricant and shock absorber in joint cavities.
• Option D: Keratan sulphate : only GAG, which does not contain uronic acid and found in cornea and tendons, thereby maintaining corneal transparency.
• Educational Objective:
• Heparan sulfate is a key component of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM) and contributes to its negative charge. This charge barrier is essential for the selective filtration of molecules based on size and charge.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A middle-aged woman who has recently experienced several episodes of diarrhea was advised by her general practitioner to consume fructans like inulin, which serve as prebiotics due to their non-digestibility. What is the primary reason for the resistance of such carbohydrates to digestion in the upper gastrointestinal tract?

explanation.short: Ans. C. Beta configuration of anomeric C2
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• The key mechanism that makes fructans like inulin function as effective prebiotics is their resistance to digestion in the upper GI tract due to the beta configuration at the anomeric C2 carbon. This structural feature creates β-glycosidic linkages between fructose units that human digestive enzymes cannot break down. As a result, these carbohydrates pass intact to the lower intestine where they selectively stimulate the growth of beneficial bacteria, particularly Bifidobacteria and Lactobacilli.

subject: Biochemistry
question: In considering the chemical structure of lactose as it pertains to its digestion and metabolism within the human body, it is essential to identify the specific type of glycosidic bond present. Which of the following best describes the glycosidic linkage found in the disaccharide lactose?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: An α (1 -> 4) glycosidic bond is found in maltose , not lactose.
• Option C: An α (1 -> 6) linkage is characteristic of the branching point in lactulose, glycogen and starch, not found in lactose.
• Option D: β (1 -> 6) - It is typically seen in Isomaltose and structural polysaccharides like cellulose.
• Educational Objective:
• Vasudevan 9/e p 77.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Amino sugars are a crucial component in various biological structures and functions. When assessing the characteristics of amino sugars, which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: Antibiotic that contains amino sugar is tetracycline
Amino group is donated by Glutamine.

explanation.short: Ans. C. Antibiotic that contains amino sugar is tetracycline
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A : Glucosamine is indeed an amino sugar, commonly found in the exoskeleton of crustaceans and in the human body as part of the structure of glycosaminoglycans.
• Option B : The precursor for the synthesis of most amino sugars is indeed glucose, which undergoes enzymatic transformation to introduce an amino group.
• Option D : In the biosynthesis of amino sugars, the amino group is typically donated by the amino acid glutamine.
• Educational Objective:
• Important amino sugars are:
• Glucose is the precursor of Amino Sugar. Amino group is denoted by Glutamine.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
By examining the molecular structures of D-Glucose and D-Galactose, these two sugars are nearly identical except for the arrangement around a single carbon atom, which classifies them as epimers. Which carbon is responsible for their epimeric relationship?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture13.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: 2nd Epimer of Glucose – Mannose
• Option B: 3rd Epimer of Glucose – Allose
• Option D: C5 is not an epimeric center between glucose and galactose, as both sugars have the same configuration at this carbon atom. Ruled out.
• Educational Objective:
• To correctly identify epimeric carbon atoms in carbohydrate chemistry, noting that epimers are sugars that differ only in the configuration around one specific carbon atom.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 70-year-old man with chronic congestive heart failure (CCF) has been prescribed digoxin, which enhances cardiac contractility and rhythm. Digoxin comprises a sugar moiety and a non-sugar (aglycone) component linked via an oxygen atom. What is the chemical classification of digoxin based on this structure?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Glycoprotein: When the carbohydrate chains are attached to a polypeptide chain it is called a proteoglycan and if the carbohydrate content is less than 10%, it is generally named as a glycoprotein.
• Option C: Oligosaccharide: Further addition of sugar groups to a Trisaccharide produces tetrasaccharides, pentasaccharides and so on, known as oligosaccharides.
• Option D: Polysaccharide: Polymerized products of many monosaccharide units.
• Homoglycans ( single kind of monosaccharides, e.g. starch, glycogen and cellulose) Heteroglycans ( two or more different monosaccharides, e.g. hyaluronic acid, chondroitin sulphate).
• Educational Objective:
• The chemical structure of digoxin is a cardiac glycoside , which has therapeutic effects in heart failure by increasing the force of cardiac muscle contraction.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A one-month-old infant exhibits severe abdominal distension, pain, and diarrhea after breastfeeding. Urinalysis indicates the presence of a reducing sugar. The subsequent test yields the crystal formation shown in the image. What type of crystals are these?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/03/30/picture15.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Glucosazone and B: Fructosazone: The differences in glucose , fructose and mannose are dependent on the first and second carbon atoms , and when the Osazone is formed these differences are masked . Hence, these 3 sugars will produce the same needle-shaped crystals arranged like sheaves of corn or a broom.
• Option D: Maltosazone: sunflower or petal shaped.
• Educational Objective:
• Lactose intolerance or lactase deficiency in an infant, diagnosed by the presence of lactosazone crystals in the urine following the ingestion of lactose from breast milk.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A clinician is explaining the importance of various glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) in the human body to a group of medical students. One topic of discussion is the essential role certain GAGs play in maintaining corneal transparency, a crucial property for proper vision. Which GAG is primarily responsible for this function?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Chondroitin sulphate: present in the ground substance of connective tissues which is distributed in cartilage, bone, tendons, cornea, and skin.
• Option C: Dermatan sulphate: Found in skin, blood vessels and heart valves and determines the elasticity of heart valve and skin.
• Option D: Hyaluronic acid: present in connective tissues, tendons, synovial fluid and vitreous humor and acts as a lubricant and shock absorber in joint cavities.
• Educational Objective:
• Keratan sulphate is the GAG that plays a significant role in maintaining corneal transparency . It is highly sulfated , which facilitates the arrangement of collagen fibers in the cornea in a uniformly spaced pattern , essential for the cornea's transparency.
• Harper 30/e:  p 638, Lippincott 7/e: P 159

subject: Biochemistry
question: In the structural hierarchy of proteoglycans, which are crucial to the extracellular matrix's organization, one particular component forms the backbone to which the protein-bound glycosaminoglycan (GAG) chains are attached. This backbone allows the proteoglycan to maintain its structure and function. Which of the following is the backbone component of proteoglycan molecules?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Heparan sulphate is a type of glycosaminoglycan that binds to core proteins but is not the backbone itself.
• Option B: Keratan sulphate is another glycosaminoglycan present on proteoglycans but again does not serve as the backbone.
• Option D: Dermatan sulphate is a glycosaminoglycan that is typically found linked to a protein as part of a proteoglycan but is not the central backbone molecule.
• Educational Objective:
• Hyaluronic acid is the backbone of proteoglycan aggregates in the extracellular matrix . It provides a central core that other proteoglycans attach to non-covalently, forming large , complex structures that contribute to the matrix's function .
• Vasudevan 9/e p 80.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 6-year-old boy is evaluated for joint stiffness and gait difficulties that have persisted for the last three months. Additionally, he is experiencing visual disturbances. On examination, the patient exhibits hepatosplenomegaly, coarse facial features including a short neck, low-set ears, a depressed nasal bridge, and is found to have a low IQ. Urinary tests reveal the presence of heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate, and a fibroblast assay indicates a deficiency of the enzyme L-Iduronidase. Based on these findings, the child has been diagnosed with mucopolysaccharidosis (MPS). Which specific type of MPS is indicated by these clinical and laboratory findings?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Maroteaux-Lamy disease (MPS VI):
• Deficiency of N-Acetylgalactosamine-4-sulfatase (Arylsulfatase B) Clinical features: Skeletal deformities, corneal opacity, normal intelligence
• Option B: Hunter's disease (MPS II):
• Deficiency of iduronate sulfatase Clinical features: Developmental delay, skeletal deformities, hearing loss No corneal clouding (important distinguishing feature) X-linked recessive inheritance
• Option D: Scheie's disease (MPS I-S, milder form of MPS I):
• Partial Deficiency of α-L-Iduronidase Clinical features: Normal intelligence, mild skeletal changes, corneal opacity
• Educational Objective

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 2-year-old Boy was brought by his parents with delayed developmental milestones and pain in lower limbs and difficulty in walking for 3 months. O/E: child had stiffness of joints in both upper and lower limbs and has coarse facial features, low IQ and hepatomegaly, no corneal clouding. Urinary examination: presence of heparan sulfate and dermatan sulphate; fibroblast study: Deficiency of enzyme iduronate sulfatase. What is your diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: Maroteaux-Lamy disease : Deficiency of N-Acetyl-β D Galactosamine- 4- Sulfatase, C/F- Skeletal deformity, corneal opacity, No MR and Dermatan Sulfate in urine.
• Option C: Hurler’s disease : Is due to the deficiency of enzyme α-L Iduronidase causes accumulation of dermatan sulphate and heparan sulphate. C/F: Mental retardation, coarse facial features, hepatosplenomegaly, cloudy cornea
• Option D: Scheie’s disease : Deficiency of L-Iduronidase, C/F- No MR with mild skeletal changes, corneal opacity and DS in urine.
• Educational Objective

subject: Biochemistry
question: In individuals experiencing hemolysis, the long-term control of blood sugar levels cannot be accurately assessed using glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) due to the shortened lifespan of red blood cells. Instead, another biomarker must be utilized. Fructosamine, which refers to glycated serum proteins such as albumin, has been suggested as an alternative measure. Is the statement that fructosamine is a glycated albumin true or false?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Fructosamine is a measure of glycemic control that can be used as an alternative to HbA1c, particularly in situations where red blood cell turnover is increased , such as hemolysis . It specifically reflects the glycation of serum proteins like albumin over a shorter time frame.
• Vasudevan 9/e p 330.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Among the statements provided below, each delves into different aspects of stereochemistry and carbohydrate chemistry. Identify the statement that is accurately described.
options: Equimolar mixture of optical isomers are called as Racemic Mixture
Mutarotation is a property of C2 atom
The penultimate carbon atom in Glucose is C-2

explanation.short: Ans. A) Equimolar mixture of optical isomers are called as Racemic Mixture
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: Fructose is part of what is termed "invert sugar" when combined with glucose , especially after the hydrolysis of sucrose . The statement is misleading as it implies fructose alone is invert sugar, which is not accurate.
• Option C: Mutarotation is the change in the optical rotation due to the change in the equilibrium between two anomers , often related to the anomeric carbon, which in glucose and many other sugars is C1, not C2.
• Option D: The penultimate carbon atom in glucose , a six-carbon sugar (hexose), is C-5, not C-2.
• Educational Objective:
• Racemic Mixture : Equimolar mixture of optical isomers which has no net rotation of plane polarized light . Sucrose is Otherwise Called Invert Sugar. Sucrose is dextrorotatory and on hydrolysis it yields a mixture of dextrorotatory Glucose and levorotatory Fructose. Because of the strong levorotatory nature of fructose, Sucrose on hydrolysis becomes levorotatory. Hence, Sucrose is called invert sugar.
• Vasudevan 9/e p 71.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following pairs represent enantiomers?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• D and L Isomerism [Enantiomers]
• ↳ Difference in the orientation of the H and OH group around the Penultimate Carbon atom results in two mirror images known as D and L isomers (Enantiomers).
• ↳ orientation of the H and OH group
• ↳ Penultimate Carbon atom
• ↳ two mirror images
• ↳ D and L isomers (Enantiomers).
• ↳ The penultimate carbon atom is named as Reference Carbon atom.
• ↳ Reference Carbon atom.
• ↳ The penultimate carbon atom in Glucose and Fructose is C-5.
• ↳ Glucose and Fructose is C-5.
• ↳ Most of the naturally occurring Monosaccharides are D isomers.
• ↳ Examples of Enantiomers
• ↳ D-Glucose and L-Glucose D-Fructose and L-Fructose D-Mannose and L-Mannose D-Glyceraldehyde and L-Glyceraldehyde.
• ↳ D-Fructose and L-Fructose
• ↳ D-Mannose and L-Mannose
• ↳ D-Glyceraldehyde and L-Glyceraldehyde.
• ↳ Ref: Harper 30/e p153.
• ↳ Harper 30/e p153.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A clinician evaluates a patient with decreased visual acuity and diagnoses cataracts, a condition characterized by clouding of the lens. The development of cataracts can be influenced by various metabolic factors, including the accumulation of certain substances within the lens. Among the following, which substance's accumulation is most commonly associated with the formation of cataracts?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: While high blood glucose levels are a risk factor for cataract development , glucose itself does not accumulate in the lens to the extent that it directly causes cataracts. Instead, its conversion to sorbitol is the main issue.
• Option B: Galactose can lead to cataracts, primarily in conditions like galactosemia, where there's an inability to metabolize galactose properly , leading to its accumulation in various tissues, including the lens . This option is also plausible but less common than the accumulation of sorbitol.
• Option C: Sugar amines are not directly associated with cataract formation . While glycation of lens proteins by sugars can contribute to cataractogenesis, the accumulation of specific sugar amines is not a well-recognized cause.
• Educational Objective:
• In Diabetes mellitus, in the lens by polyol pathway, glucose converted to sorbitol by the enzyme Aldose reductase In galactosemia, Dulcitol or Galactitol is responsible for cataract Sorbitol , Dulcitol are sugar alcohols.

subject: Biochemistry
question: In the study of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) or mucopolysaccharides, understanding the structural components is crucial. Most GAGs are composed of repeating disaccharide units that include an amino sugar and often a uronic acid. However, one of the GAGs listed below is unique because it does not contain a uronic acid residue in its structure. Which GAG is this?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Heparan sulphate: It is seen in the basement membrane of the kidney with type IV collagen and laminin and plays a major role in determining the charge selectiveness of glomerular filtration
• Option B: Hyaluronic acid: Present in connective tissues , tendons , synovial fluid and vitreous humor and acts as a lubricant and shock absorber in joint cavities
• Option C: Dermatan sulphate: Found in skin, blood vessels and heart valves and determines the elasticity of heart valve and skin.
• Educational Objective
• Only GAG, which does not contain uronic acid and found in cornea and tendons and helps in maintaining corneal transparency. GAG with no Uronic Acid- Keratan Sulfate GAG with no Sulfate group- Hyaluronic Acid GAG not covalently linked to Protein- Hyaluronic Acid.
• Harper 30/e: p 638, Lippincott 6/e: p 159.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Glycogenin is often discussed in the context of glycogen synthesis, but there is a common misconception about its nature. Considering the biochemical role of glycogenin, is it accurate to classify it as a polysaccharide?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Glycogenin is a 37 kDa protein (POLYPEPTIDE). Glycogenin catalyses transfer of 7 glucosyl residues from UDPGlucose, in alpha 1 → 4 linkage. Thus, glucosyl residues are added on specific tyrosine residues of Glycogenin. Glycogenin remains at the core of the Glycogen granule .

subject: Biochemistry
question: Cellulose, a major component of plant cell walls, plays a critical role in providing structural support. Given its biological significance and chemical composition, how is cellulose best categorized?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Cellulose is not a lipoprotein ; it does not contain lipid or protein components.
• Option B: It is not a starch polysaccharide. Starch, another plant polysaccharide , is composed of α-glucose units and serves as an energy storage molecule, whereas cellulose serves a structural role.
• Option D: Cellulose is not a glycoprotein ; it does not contain any protein or covalently bound oligosaccharide chains.
• Educational Objective:
• Foods contain a variety of other polysaccharides known as nonstarch polysaccharides. They are not digested by human enzymes and form the major components of dietary fibre. E.g.: cellulose from plant cell walls (a glucose polymer) and Inulin , the storage carbohydrate in some plants (a fructose polymer).

subject: Biochemistry
question: Dextran, a complex branched polysaccharide, has been utilized in various medical applications. One specific use of dextran is in the management of hypovolemia. Based on this application, is it accurate to consider dextran as a plasma volume expander?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Dextran is indeed used as a plasma volume expander in clinical settings. By increasing the osmotic pressure in the blood vessels , it helps to retain fluid within the circulatory system , thereby expanding plasma volume. This is particularly useful in treating hypovolemia or shock.
• Vasudevan 9/e p 79.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 6-month-old boy begins experiencing vomiting, night sweats, and tremors after the introduction of fruit juices into his diet during weaning. Physical examination reveals significant hepatomegaly. Urine tests are positive for reducing sugars but negative for glucose. Which enzyme deficiency is most likely responsible for these symptoms?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A.  Galactokinase: Deficiency in galactokinase leads to galactosemia type 2, which primarily results in cataract formation but does not typically cause severe hypoglycemia or hepatomegaly in response to fructose ingestion.
• Option B. Fructokinase: Fructokinase deficiency leads to essential fructosuria, a benign condition characterized by the excretion of fructose in the urine without serious metabolic disturbances or clinical symptoms.
• Option D. β-Galactosidase: Deficiency in β-galactosidase leads to GM1 gangliosidosis or galactosialidosis , which involve neurological symptoms and developmental delays , but not the specific reaction to fructose ingestion as described.
• Educational Objective:
• Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI) is a condition resulting from aldolase B deficiency , characterized by adverse reactions to fructose ingestion, such as hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, and metabolic disturbances.

subject: Biochemistry
question: There are one specific vitamins, which is involved in the TCA cycle as key for comprehending its biochemical intricacies and the nutritional requirements that support this fundamental metabolic pathway. Which of the following vitamins are involved in the TCA cycle?
options: Pantothenic Acid as a part of CoA

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pantothenic Acid: It is a component of Coenzyme A (CoA) , essential for the formation of Acetyl- CoA, a substrate entering the TCA cycle.
• Option B. Riboflavin: It is converted into Flavin Adenine Dinucleotide (FAD), a coenzyme used by succinate dehydrogenase in the TCA cycle.
• Option C. Thiamine: As thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) , it is a coenzyme for the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex that converts pyruvate into Acetyl-CoA, linking glycolysis with the TCA cycle.
• Educational Objective:
• Vitamins in TCA Cycle:
• Pantothenic Acid as a part of CoA Riboflavin as FAD Thiamine Niacin as NAD+

subject: Biochemistry
question: A research team is exploring the metabolic adaptations of cancer cells and how these cells meet their increased energy demands. In most cancer cells, the predominant metabolic pathway for generating the necessary energy is distinct from that of normal, non-cancerous cells. Which metabolic process do cancer cells primarily exploit to derive the energy needed for their rapid growth and proliferation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Oxidative phosphorylation : While important in many tissues, cancer cells rely less on oxidative phosphorylation compared to normal cells , partially because it is less efficient under hypoxic conditions, which are common in tumors.
• Option C. TCA cycle in mitochondria : The TCA cycle is used by all cells , but cancer cells often have mitochondrial defects that reduce their reliance on this pathway.
• Option D. Beta-oxidation : This pathway oxidizes fatty acids to generate acetyl-CoA for energy ; however, cancer cells typically rely on this less than on glycolysis.
• Educational Objective:
• Cancer cells predominantly use glycolysis for energy production, even in the presence of oxygen, a phenomenon known as the Warburg effect. This metabolic adaptation allows cancer cells to thrive even under hypoxic conditions and contributes to the acidic microenvironment often seen in tumors due to lactate production.

subject: Biochemistry
question: During fasting, the body employs various mechanisms to maintain blood glucose levels. Which of the following sequences correctly represents the order in which these mechanisms are activated to ensure blood glucose homeostasis in the fasting state?

explanation.short: Ans. A) Glucose from diet > glycogenolysis > gluconeogenesis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Glucose from diet > gluconeogenesis > glycogenolysis: This sequence is incorrect because glycogenolysis typically precede s gluconeogenesis in the fasting state as the body utilizes stored glycogen before synthesizing glucose from non-carbohydrate sources.
• Option C. Glucose from diet > gluconeogenesis > proteolysis: While proteolysis (the breakdown of proteins ) does contribute substrates for gluconeogenesis , it is not a direct mechanism for maintaining blood glucose levels but rather provides amino acids that can be used in gluconeogenesis.
• Option D. Glucose from diet > lipolysis > gluconeogenesis: Lipolysis, the breakdown of triglycerides into free fatty acids and glycerol , does provide substrates for gluconeogenesis (specifically glycerol), but it primarily serves as a mechanism for providing energy through fatty acid oxidation rather than directly contributing to maintaining blood glucose levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Liver produces glucose by glycogenolysis and later by gluconeogenesis so that the obligatory requirements of the brain are met. Liver also produces the ketone bodies , an alternate source of fuel. But the liver cannot use ketone bodies as its own fuel In the fasting state 2-2 1/2 hours – glucose from meals Next 3 hour s – from glycogenolysis Later from gluconeogenesis (proteolysis & lipolysis) Hyperglycemic hormones are secreted
• Liver is the net producer of glucose in the fasting state

subject: Biochemistry
question: A medical student is reviewing the glycolytic pathway and is attempting to identify the points of regulation within the process. She notes that certain steps are irreversible and thus play a key role in the regulation of glycolysis. Which of the following enzymes catalyzes a reaction in glycolysis that is not considered irreversible and therefore not a major regulatory point?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option B. Phosphofructokinase : Catalyzes an irreversible step by converting fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate and is a major regulatory point of glycolysis.
• Option C. Pyruvate Kinase : Irreversibly Catalyzes the conversion of phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvate, another regulatory step in glycolysis.
• Option D. Hexokinase : Irreversibly phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate at the start of the glycolytic pathway and is allosterically inhibited by its product, making it a key regulatory enzyme.
• Educational Objective:
• Irreversible Steps of Glycolysis:
• Hexokinase Phosphofructokinase Pyruvate Kinase

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 55-year-old man presents with fatigue, abdominal pain, and hepatomegaly, with abnormal liver function tests suggesting ethanol toxicity. What could be the underlying biochemical change associated with ethanol toxicity in this scenario?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Decreased lactate/pyruvate ratio: Ethanol metabolism actually leads to an increased lactate/pyruvate ratio due to the conversion of pyruvate to lactate in the presence of excess NADH.
• Option C. Stimulation of gluconeogenesis: Ethanol metabolism inhibits gluconeogenesis due to the increased NADH/NAD+ ratio and the subsequent diversion of substrates like pyruvate and oxaloacetate to lactate and malate , respectively.
• Option D. Stimulation of fatty acid oxidation: Ethanol metabolism inhibits fatty acid oxidation , again due to the increased NADH/NAD+ ratio. Instead, there is an accumulation of fatty acids leading to fatty liver.
• Educational Objective:
• Both the oxidation steps in alcohol metabolism produce NADH, resulting in a high NADH/NAD+ ratio. As a result, the following metabolic adaptations occur:
• Lactic acidosis. Hypoglycemia. Ketogenesis. Fatty liver and steatosis. Mitochondrial damage and apoptosis. CNS depression

subject: Biochemistry
question: A teenager is brought to the ER with symptoms of increased confusion and lethargy. Lab tests reveal abnormal liver function and significantly elevated serum ammonia levels. His parents note his recent high protein intake, attributed to his athletic activities. Hyperammonemia can disrupt the TCA cycle by depleting a specific intermediate, leading to impaired energy metabolism. Which TCA cycle intermediate is most likely depleted in this scenario, contributing to the cycle's inhibition?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Hyperammonemia can i nhibit the TCA cycle by depleting α-ketoglutarate . Ammonia is detoxified in the liver by the urea cycle, which consumes α-ketoglutarate as it combines with ammonia to form glutamate. This process depletes α-ketoglutarate , a critical intermediate in the TCA cycle , leading to impaired energy metabolism and contributing to the symptoms observed.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Garrod's tetrad refers to a group of four metabolic disorders. Which of the following options correctly lists these disorders?

explanation.short: Ans. A)  Alkaptonuria, Pentosuria, Cystinuria, Albinism
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Alkaptonuria, Pentos Uria, Galactosemia, Albinism: While galactosemia is a well-known metabolic disorder, it is not part of Garrod's original tetrad.
• Option C. Fructosuria, Pentosuria, Cystinuria, Albinism: Fructosuria is another metabolic condition but was not included in Garrod's tetrad.
• Option D. Alkaptonuria, Pentos Uria, Cystinuria, Mucopolysaccharidosis: Mucopolysaccharidosis refers to a group of lysosomal storage diseases and is not part of Garrod's tetrad.
• Educational Objective:
• Cystinuria Alkaptonuria Albinism Pentosuria

subject: Biochemistry
question: Regarding the metabolic effects of insulin, which of the following statements is INCORRECT?
options: It prevents lipolysis by inhibiting the activity of hormone sensitive lipase
It increases proteins synthesis and prevents protein breakdown

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Insulin facilitates diffusion of glucose in muscle. Transporter, GLUT-4 is insulin dependent
• Utilization of Glucose : Glycolysis increased (key glycolytic enzymes: glucokinase, phosphofructokinase & pyruvate kinase) Insulin favors storage of glucose: Glycogen synthesis increased (Glycogen synthase) (option B) Lipogenesis increased: Acetyl CoA Carboxylase and Glucose-6-Phosphate   dehydrogenase (NADPH) activity is enhanced by insulin. Anti-lipolytic effect: Hormone sensitive lipase is decreased (option A). Gluconeogenesis Decreased (Key gluconeogenic enzymes: Pyruvate carboxylase, Phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase, Fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase and Glucose 6-phosphatase are inhibited by insulin. (option D) Anti-ketogenic Effect : HMG CoA synthase is decreased It increases proteins synthesis and prevents protein breakdown

subject: Biochemistry
question: Identify which of the following pathways does not produce high-energy phosphate compounds directly helps in comprehending the energy dynamics within the cell.

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Pathways which do not synthesize ATP are
• HMP Pathway Rapaport Luebering Cycle Uronic acid pathway Alpha oxidation of Fatty acid Omega Oxidation of fatty acid

subject: Biochemistry
question: Assertion (A): Piericidin and rotenone are inhibitors of (ETC) electron transport chain. Reasoning ®: Piericidin and rotenone inhibits the flow of electron between the complex II and ubiquinone (coenzyme Q)
options: A and R both are right but R is not the Correct explanation of A
A as well as R are right and R is a correct explanation of A
A and R both are wrong
A is true but R is false

explanation.short: Ans. D) A is right statement but R is not a correct explanation of statement A.
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Piericidin and rotenone are indeed inhibitors of the electron transport chain (ETC), specifically affecting the function of Complex I ( NADH dehydrogenase ). They block the transfer of electrons from NADH to ubiquinone ( coenzyme Q ), no t from Complex II . This action inhibits the electron transport chain early in its sequence, preventing the establishment of the proton gradient necessary for ATP synthesis via oxidative phosphorylation . The assertion is correct in stating that these compounds are inhibitors of the ETC , but the reasoning is incorrect as it misidentifies the site of inhibition .
• Educational Objective:

subject: Biochemistry
question: In the context of glucose metabolism and insulin action, GLUT4 is characterized as a glucose transporter. Is it accurate to state that GLUT4's activity is regulated by insulin?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• GLUT4 is the major glucose transporter seen in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue . GLUT4 is under the control of insulin . Insulin promotes the translocation of intracellular GLUT4 molecules to the cell surface and thus increases glucose uptake. In Type 2 diabetes mellitus , membrane GLUT4 is reduced causing insulin resistance in muscle and fat cells. In diabetes, entry of glucose into muscle is only half of normal cells.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 5-months-old baby girl was given cow’s milk at her 5 weeks and after 3 weeks she was admitted with a history of screaming after feeding and was discharged on advice to avoid cow’s milk. Again, worsening of symptoms such as screaming, gas formation and distended abdomen occurred after she drank juice. Blood investigations, liver biopsy and physical examination were all normal. If an intestinal tissue biopsy is done and analyzed from the patient, which of the following would be deficient or defective?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. SGLT: Sodium Dependent Glucose Transporter-1 (SGLT-1) is the transporter in the intestine involved in glucose-galactose malabsorption . For diarrhoea , ORS is given which contains both glucose and sodium → allows absorption of sodium to replenish body sodium chloride levels and glucose to provide energy.
• Option C. Isomaltose: he cells of brush border of intestine contain sucrase, maltase, Isomaltose and lactase → which hydrolyze the corresponding disaccharides into monosaccharides , which are then absorbed.
• Option D.  Lactase: Deficiency of lactase leads to lactose intolerance → accumulation of lactose in the gut → Irritant diarrhoea and flatulence. Treatment- withdrawn milk temporarily.
• Educational Objective:
• GLUT 5 is a fructose transporter expressed in the intestinal mucosa. A deficiency or defect in GLUT 5 would lead to fructose malabsorption , presenting with symptoms such as abdominal pain, distension, and gas formation after consuming fructose.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 58-year-old male with type 2 diabetes starts a new oral hypoglycemic regimen. Soon after, he presents with anxiety, diaphoresis, and hunger, which quickly resolves upon consuming glucose. This clinical scenario underscores the importance of the initial enzymatic step in glycolysis following glucose ingestion. In the context of glycolytic pathway activation for immediate energy production, which enzyme is responsible for catalyzing the first reaction that glucose undergoes in this metabolic pathway?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Aldolase and Pyruvate kinase : These enzymes function in the intermediate to later stages of glycolysis and are not involved in the initial phosphorylation of glucose.
• Option B. Phosphofructokinase : This enzyme plays a key regulatory role in glycolysis by converting fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate , but it is not the first enzyme in the pathway.
• Option D. Phosphoglucose isomerase : This enzyme acts second in the glycolytic pathway , converting glucose-6-phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate , following the action of hexokinase.
• Educational Objective:
• Hexokinase catalyzes the first critical step in the glycolytic pathway by phosphorylating glucose to form glucose-6-phosphate . This reaction is essential for trapping glucose within the cell, enabling its subsequent metabolism in the glycolytic pathway to produce energy, particularly important in the context of restoring normal blood glucose levels after hypoglycemic episodes in diabetic patients.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A 42-year-old marathon runner experiences muscle fatigue and laboured breathing post-race. Upon evaluation, a metabolic cycle is considered in understanding her physiological state, as shown below. Which statement is correct regarding the metabolic cycle illustrated?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/01/picture1_XU5jYM8.jpg]
options: No re-utilization of lactate for gluconeogenesis
Energy need explains the oxygen debt after exercise

explanation.short: Ans. D) Energy need explains the oxygen debt after exercise
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Cahill’s cycle : It is also know n as glucose-alanine cycle and occurs in fasting muscles .
• Option B. Location not seen in RBCs : Lactate is released by exercising skeletal muscle and by cells that lack mitochondria such as RBCs into the blood.
• Option C. No re-utilization of lactate for gluconeogenesis : Glucose is converted to lactate in the muscle and in the liver this lactate is re-converted into glucose.
• Educational Objective:
• The Cori cycle is energy-intensive, costing 6 ATPs in the liver to resynthesize glucose from 2 lactate molecules . During exercise , the body incurs an " oxygen debt ," as it uses more oxygen to replenish ATP stores and clear the lactate produced, explaining the labored breathing post-exercise.

subject: Biochemistry
question: In the context of infantile conditions, hypoglycemia is observed in which of the following?

explanation.short: Ans. C. Both a and b
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Both Glycogen Storage Disease Type I (GSD I) and Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI) can lead to hypoglycemia , particularly in infants.
• Glycogen Storage Disease Type I (Von Gierke Disease)
• Caused by a deficiency of glucose-6-phosphatase . This enzyme is crucial for the final step of gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis , converting glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose. Without it, the liver cannot release glucose into the bloodstream → severe fasting hypoglycemia , typically noticed shortly after birth.
• Option B: Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI)
• Caused by a deficiency of aldolase B . When fructose, sucrose, or sorbitol is introduced (e.g., via formula or weaning), fructose-1-phosphate accumulates , which: Traps phosphate Inhibits glycogenolysis Leads to hypoglycemia and other symptoms like vomiting , hepatomegaly , and failure to thrive .
• Note : HFI typically manifests after exposure to fructose-containing foods , so it may not present immediately after birth , but it is a cause of hypoglycemia in infants , especially during weaning.
• Educational Objective:
• Be aware of metabolic disorders that can cause hypoglycemia in neonates or infants , particularly:
• Glycogen Storage Disease Type I (Von Gierke) Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI) Galactosemia Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency (MCAD) Long-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency (LCAD)

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following laboratory findings are associated with chronic alcoholism?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Elevated GGT (Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase) activity: GGT is an enzyme involved in the metabolism of glutathione and is often elevated in alcohol-related liver disease . Its elevation is commonly used as a biomarker for excessive alcohol consumption and liver damage.
• Option B. Elevated ALT (Alanine Aminotransferase) activity: ALT is an enzyme found predominantly in the liver . Its elevation in the blood often indicates liver damage , which can occur as a result of chronic alcohol abuse.
• Option C. Decreased ADH (Antidiuretic Hormone) activity: Chronic alcohol consumption can lead to impaired ADH secretion or function , contributing to electrolyte imbalances and an increased risk of dehydration.
• Considering these points, option D) All the above is correct, as each listed factor can be associated with chronic alcoholism and its impact on the body.
• Options A, B, and C are individually correct but D encompasses all the possible findings, making it the most comprehensive choice.
• Educational Objective:
• Increase in serum levels of gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) and alanine transaminase (ALT) Decrease in aldehyde dehydrogenase activity (in liver cells and RBCs ) - best marker for alcohol abuse. Disialotransferrin ( sialic acid groups are removed from transferrin ) level in blood- highly sensitive marker for chronic alcohol abuse.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A patient with an established diagnosis of diabetes mellitus reports to the clinic for a follow-up, presenting with symptoms of fatigue, weight loss, polyuria, and a burning sensation in the soles. Despite ongoing treatment, his blood glucose levels are significantly elevated. Among the following options, which is LEAST likely to contribute to the hyperglycemia seen in uncontrolled Type 1 diabetes?

explanation.short: Ans. D) Increased skeletal muscle Glycogenolysis
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Decreased skeletal muscle glucose uptake : This is a significant contributor to hyperglycaemia in Type 1 diabetes , as insulin deficiency prevents glucose from entering cells, leading to elevated blood glucose levels.
• Option B. Decreased adipose lipogenesis: While decreased formation of fat from glucose in adipose tissue could be observed in insulin deficiency, it's not a direct contributor to elevated glucose levels. Insulin promotes lipogenesis , so its deficiency would decrease this process, but this decrease does not directly cause hyperglycemia.
• Option C. Increased adipose lipolysis: Increased breakdown of fat in the absence of insulin action elevates free fatty acid levels, which can exacerbate insulin resistance and contribute to higher blood glucose levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Skeletal muscle cannot export glucose into the circulation like liver due to absence of enzyme glucose-6-phosphatase. So, intramyocellular glycogen acts as a fuel source for skeletal muscle and doesn’t contribute to the hyperglycaemia in uncontrolled type I diabetes.
• Reasons of hyperglycemia associated with uncontrolled type I diabetes:
• There will be decreased insulin mediated glucose utilization by skeletal muscle and adipose tissues → will contribute to hyperglycaemia. There will also be decreased insulin-mediated suppression of hepatic glucose output. Decreased insulin-mediated suppression of lipolysis → hyperglycaemia , by activation of gluconeogenesis by fatty acid breakdown product acetyl CoA

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 6-month-old girl presents with developing cataracts, and an inability to track objects visually. Urine tests are positive for reducing sugars but negative for glucose. Which enzyme deficiency is most likely present in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Distinguish between types of galactose metabolism disorders :
• Galactokinase deficiency : Early-onset cataracts , galactosuria , mild/no systemic symptoms. Classic galactosemia (GALT deficiency) : Severe symptoms including hepatomegaly , vomiting , jaundice , and cataracts . Both cause reducing substances in urine , but galactokinase deficiency is less severe and lacks liver involvement .

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 4-year-old boy experiences headache, dizziness, weakness, and knee joint tenderness after consuming sugarcane juice. Following a diagnosis of Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI), which of the following statements about HFI is FALSE?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI) is caused by a deficiency of aldolase B, the enzyme that cleaves fructose 1-phosphate into dihydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) and glyceraldehyde.
• In HFI: Fructose 1-phosphate accumulates in tissues (especially the liver and       kidneys)leading to: Inhibition of glycogenolysis Resulting in hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), especially after ingestion of fructose, sucrose, or sorbitol.
• Hypoglycemia, vomiting, lethargy, abdominal pain after fructose intake. Long-term exposure can cause hepatomegaly, liver dysfunction, and renal involvement.
• Option A. Deficiency of aldolase B: True. This is the fundamental enzymatic defect in HFI. Option B. Accumulation of fructose 1-phosphate in tissues: True. This is the hallmark of the disease and leads to metabolic blockages. Option D. Liver and kidneys are involved: True. These organs are significantly affected due to accumulation of fructose 1-phosphate and its toxic effects.
• Educational Objective:
• Biochemical defect in Hereditary Fructose Intolerance
• Deficiency of Fructose-1,6-Bisphosphate Aldolase (Aldolase B)
• Deficiency of this enzyme → causes accumulation of fructose-1-phosphate → severe toxic symptoms when a patient is exposed to fructose.
• Patients with HFI are asymptomatic until fructose or sucrose is ingested (usually from fruit, fruit juice, or sweetened cereal).
• Symptoms may occur early in life, soon after weaning formulas containing these sugars are introduced into the diet.
• Early clinical manifestations include jaundice, hepatomegaly, vomiting, lethargy, irritability, convulsions and hypoglycaemia.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Assertion (A): A nursing mother with classic Galactosemia is on a galactose-free diet and she is able to produce lactose in breast milk. Reasoning (R): Galactose can be produced from a glucose metabolite by epimerization:
options: A is right but R is a wrong statement
A as well as R are right and R is a correct explanation of A
A is wrong but R is a right statement
A and R both are wrong

explanation.short: Ans. B) A as well as R are right, and R is a correct explanation of A
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. A is right but R is a wrong statement: This option is incorrect because both the assertion and the reasoning are correct, and the reasoning provides a valid explanation for the assertion.
• Option C. A is wrong, but R is a right statement: This option is incorrect because the assertion is indeed correct; nursing mothers with classic galactosemia on a galactose-free die t can produce lactose in breast milk due to the body's ability to synthesize galactose from glucose.
• Option D. A and R both are wrong: This option is incorrect as both the assertion and the reasoning are correct and logically consistent.
• Educational Objective:
• Galactose can be produced from a glucose metabolite by epimerization. Uridine diphosphate (UDP) glucose is converted to UDP-galactose by UDP hexose 4- epimerase , thereby providing the appropriate form of galactose for lactose synthesis.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
A 42-year-old visits a local clinic for a routine check-up. The healthcare provider at the clinic performs an Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT) to assess his glucose metabolism. The results are plotted on a graph showing plasma glucose levels over time after a glucose load. Based on the graph shown, what is your interpretation of the patient’s OGTT results?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/Picture2%20(2).jpg]
options: Symptoms of Diabetes mellitus with inconclusive blood glucose values
Follow-up of a case of Diabetes mellitus

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Based on the graph provided, which shows plasma glucose levels after a glucose load:
• The normal curve rises to about 140 mg/dL and then returns to baseline (~90 mg/dL) within 2 hours. The diabetic curve spikes up to ~300 mg/dL at 1 hour and remains significantly elevated (well above 200 mg/dL) even at 2 hours. the Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT) is interpreted as follows: Normal: Fasting glucose < 100 mg/dL; 2-hour post-load < 140 mg/dL. Impaired glucose tolerance: fasting glucose 100-126 mg/dl; 2-hour post-load 140–199 mg/dL. Diabetes mellitus: Fasting ≥ 126 mg/dL or 2-hour post-load ≥ 200 mg/dL .
• The patient's OGTT pattern on the graph matches the diabetic curve (persistent elevation above 200 mg/dL even after 2 hours), clearly meeting criteria for overt diabetes mellitus.
• Option A. Symptoms of Diabetes mellitus with inconclusive blood glucose values : This applies when symptoms are present but glucose levels don’t conclusively meet diagnostic thresholds (not the case here, where the glucose is clearly high). Option C. Diabetic nephropathy : This is a complication of diabetes involving kidney damage and is not diagnosed by OGTT . OGTT assesses glucose metabolism, not kidney function. Option D. Follow-up of a case of Diabetes mellitus : OGTT is not recommended for follow-up of known diabetes cases. HbA1c, fasting glucose, and self-monitoring of blood glucose are the preferred methods for monitoring glycemic control in diagnosed patients.
• Educational Objectives:
• Difference between normal, impaired, and diabetic glucose values.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 45-year-old male presents with symptoms of excessive thirst, increased appetite, tiredness, and a thin build. He has been advised to undergo fasting blood sugar (FBS) and postprandial blood sugar (PPBS) tests and is currently 12 hours into his fast. During this period of fasting, which one of the following enzymes is likely to be phosphorylated and active?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Arginase: Arginase is an enzyme involved in the urea cycle , which converts arginine into urea and ornithine . Its activity is not directly influenced by fasting or feeding states .
• Option B. Fatty acid synthase: Fatty acid synthase is involved in the synthesis of fatty acids , a process that is downregulated during fasting when the body is mobilizing , rather than storing, energy.
• Option C. Glycogen synthase: Glycogen synthase is responsible for glycogen synthesis in the liver and muscle cells . This enzyme is less active during fasting , as the body is breaking down glycogen stores, not building them.
• Educational Objective:
• Hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL) is an enzyme in adipose tissue that breaks down stored triglycerides into free fatty acids and glycerol , which can then be used for energy . HSL is activated by phosphorylation in response to low insulin and high glucagon levels , which are typical during fasting . Thus, in a fasting state , HSL would be phosphorylated and active to facilitate the mobilization of fat stores .

subject: Biochemistry
question: An Infant diagnosed with Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI) presents with vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, and hypoglycemia upon attempting weaning. Following a positive IV fructose tolerance test, dietary restriction of fructose is advised. Considering the metabolic pathway affected in HFI, which of the following substances should be restricted in the neonate's diet?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• In Infants with Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI), dietary restriction of sucrose is crucial due to its breakdown into glucose and fructose, the latter of which cannot be metabolized due to aldolase B deficiency, leading to toxic metabolite buildup and hypoglycemia. Other sugars like galactose, glucose, and lactose, which do not directly contribute to these metabolic issues, do not need to be restricted unless other specific metabolic disorders are present.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 39-year-old female presents with weight loss, fatigue, nocturia, and excessive thirst. Laboratory findings reveal fasting blood sugar (FBS) >140 mg/dl and postprandial blood sugar (PPBS) >250 mg/dl, leading to a probable diagnosis of Type 1 diabetes mellitus. Considering the pathophysiology of Type 1 diabetes mellitus, which of the following statements is TRUE?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Type 1 diabetes mellitus is characterized by an autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells , leading to absolute insulin deficiency . Insulin normally inhibits lipolysis , so its deficiency in Type 1 diabetes results in increased lipolysis in adipose tissue . This leads to elevated levels of free fatty acids in the bloodstream, which can contribute to the production of ketone bodies in the liver, a common complication in uncontrolled diabetes.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 45-year-old male with Type 1 diabetes mellitus presents with a blood glucose level greater than 300 mg/dl. In the context of his elevated blood glucose, which of the following metabolic processes continues to occur normally in this patient?
options: Increased conversion of fatty acid to Acetyl CoA

explanation.short: Ans. C. Increased conversion of fatty acid to Acetyl CoA
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• In Type 1 diabetes mellitus with significantly elevated blood glucose (>300 mg/dl), insulin deficiency leads to dramatic metabolic alterations. Without insulin, glucose uptake by peripheral tissues is impaired, leading to hyperglycemia. Simultaneously, the body shifts to alternative fuel sources:
• Increased fatty acid mobilization from adipose tissue occurs in insulin deficiency. These fatty acids undergo β-oxidation in the liver, resulting in increased conversion to Acetyl CoA.
• The excess Acetyl CoA cannot enter the TCA cycle efficiently due to low oxaloacetate availability (diverted to gluconeogenesis), and instead is shunted toward ketogenesis, producing ketone bodies (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate, and acetone). This leads to diabetic ketoacidosis in uncontrolled Type 1 diabetes.
• Option A. Increased protein synthesis : Insulin promotes protein synthesis and inhibits protein breakdown. In Type 1 diabetes with insulin deficiency, protein synthesis decreases while proteolysis increases, providing amino acids for gluconeogenesis. This contributes to the catabolic state and muscle wasting seen in poorly controlled diabetes.
• Option B. Glycogenesis in muscle : This process requires both insulin and adequate glucose transport into muscle cells via GLUT4 transporters. Insulin deficiency impairs GLUT4 translocation to the cell membrane, reducing glucose uptake and subsequent glycogen synthesis in muscle tissue.
• Option D. Decreased triglyceride level - This is incorrect. In Type 1 diabetes with insulin deficiency, triglyceride levels are not decreased but are typically increased due to impaired clearance of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins due to decreased lipoprotein lipase activity (an insulin-dependent enzyme) This often leads to hypertriglyceridemia, not decreased triglyceride levels.
• Educational Objective:
• In uncontrolled Type 1 diabetes with hyperglycemia, insulin deficiency creates a catabolic state where fat mobilization and fatty acid oxidation are accelerated, leading to increased Acetyl CoA production and ketogenesis. This represents a shift from glucose to fat metabolism. Meanwhile, anabolic processes like protein synthesis and glycogenesis are impaired, highlighting the crucial role of insulin in regulating metabolic pathways.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A patient with a known history of diabetes mellitus presents to the outpatient department for a routine follow-up appointment after three months. As part of the management and monitoring plan, the healthcare provider requests an HbA1c test, which is crucial for assessing long-term glycemic control. Considering the various methods available for HbA1c estimation, which of the following is considered the most accurate and reliable method?

explanation.short: Ans. C) High performance liquid chromatography
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Affinity chromatography: While affinity chromatography can be used for HbA1c measurement, it is not as specific or as widely used as HPLC. It relies on the specific binding of HbA1c to a particular substrate, which can be influenced by the presence of hemoglobin variants.
• Option B.  Electrophoresis: Electrophoresis can separate different hemoglobin types based on their electric charge, but it is less precise than HPLC for quantifying HbA1c levels and can be affected by the presence of hemoglobin variants.
• Option D. Isoelectric focusing: This technique separates proteins based on their isoelectric points. While it can differentiate various hemoglobin forms , it is not the preferred method for routine HbA1c estimation due to its complexity and the difficulty in quantification.
• Educational Objective:
• High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) is widely recognized as the gold standard for HbA1c estimation due to its accuracy, reliability, and reproducibility . HPLC separates various forms of hemoglobin, including HbA1c, based on their charge and size under high pressure , allowing for precise quantification. This method minimizes interference from hemoglobin variants and provides a clear, specific measurement of HbA1c levels , which reflect the average blood glucose concentration over the previous 2-3 months.
explanation.ref: Tietz fundamentals of Clinical Chemistry, 6th edition, page 396

subject: Biochemistry
question: Assertion (A): Glucagon is needed for glycogenesis to occur. Reasoning (R): The enzyme glycogen phosphorylase is the rate limiting enzyme for glycogenesis and it is active in phosphorylated form for which glucagon is needed. Evaluate the accuracy of the assertion and reasoning provided.
options: A is right but R is a wrong statement
A as well as R are right and R is a correct explanation of A
A is wrong but R is a right statement
A and R both are wrong

explanation.short: Ans. D) A and R both are wrong
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• For glycogenesis, presence of insulin is must, because Insulin is required for dephosphorylation of glycogen synthase enzyme, which is the rate limiting enzyme for glycogenesis. Glycogen synthase is active in a dephosphorylated state.
• Educational Objective:
• Glycogen phosphorylase is the key enzyme in glycogenolysis, the breakdown of glycogen into glucose-1-phosphate, not glycogenesis. This enzyme is activated in its phosphorylated form, but this activation is triggered by glucagon and adrenaline during fasting or stress, not for promoting glycogen synthesis but for breaking down glycogen into glucose to raise blood glucose levels. Glycogen synthase is the key rate-limiting enzyme in glycogenesis and is regulated by insulin and glucose levels , not glucagon.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 25-year-old male presents with complaints of pain in his calves during and after physical activity. Upon further evaluation, including a detailed examination and muscle biopsy, it was noted that there was an increased glycogen accumulation in the muscle tissues. Based on these findings, which of the following enzymes is most likely deficient in this patient?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B.  Liver phosphorylase kinase: Is a type VIII disorder with hepatomegaly, accumulation of glycogen in liver and mild hypoglycaemia.
• Option C. Glucose 6 phosphatase: Is deficient in Von Gierke disease (Ia), C/F includes glycogen accumulation in liver and renal tubule cells, hypoglycaemia, lactic academia, ketosis and hyperlipemia. (No muscle weakness).
• Option D.  Branching enzyme: Is defective in Andersen disease with Hepatosplenomegaly ; accumulation of polysaccharide with few branch points ; death from heart or liver failure before age 5.
• Educational Objective:

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 20-year-old male with a history of short stature presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, and bleeding tendencies. On examination, the patient also has notable hepatomegaly and enlarged kidneys. Laboratory findings include elevated levels of lactate, cholesterol, triglycerides, and uric acid. Considering the constellation of symptoms and lab findings, which enzyme deficiency is most likely responsible for this patient's clinical presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A.  Branching enzyme: deficiency results in Anderson disease . C/F: failure to thrive, liver failure, and hypotonia.
• Option B.  Debranching enzyme: deficiency results in Cori disease . C/F: hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, short-stature, and myopathy.
• Option C. Muscle phosphorylase: deficiency results in McArdle disease. C/F: pain, cramps and myoglobinuria on strenuous exercise.
• Educational Objective:
• Von Gierke's disease (Type I glycogen storage disease) , characterized by a deficiency in glucose-6-phosphatase. This enzyme is crucial for the final steps of gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis, converting glucose-6-phosphate into free glucose . Its deficiency leads to impaired glucose production , resulting in hypoglycemia, and the accumulation of glycogen and fat in the liver and kidneys, causing hepatomegaly and renal enlargement . The increased levels of lactate, cholesterol, triglycerides, and uric acid are also consistent with the metabolic disturbances seen in von Gierke's disease.

subject: Biochemistry
question: In a pediatric grand rounds presentation, a challenging case involving a 6-month-old infant with recurrent episodes of early morning hypoglycemia is discussed. The attending physician explains that administering glucagon raised the infant's blood glucose levels when fed but was ineffective during fasting periods. A liver biopsy revealed abnormal glycogen accumulation. The audience, consisting of pediatricians JRs, is then asked to determine the enzyme deficiency responsible for the infant's condition based on the clinical findings?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B. Muscle phosphorylase : Type V – McArdle’s syndrome; Poor exercise tolerance with high muscle glycogen and low blood lactate after exercise.
• Option C. Glucose-6-phosphatase: Is deficient in Von Gierke disease (Ia) ,C/F includes glycogen accumulation in liver and renal t ubule cells , hypoglycaemia, lactic academia , ketosis and hyperlipidemia.
• Option D. Branching enzyme: Is defective in Anderson disease with Hepatosplenomegaly; accumulation of polysaccharide with few branch points; death from heart or liver failure before age 5.
• Educational Objective:
• The clinical features of Type III Glycogen Storage Disease (Cori's or Forbes' Disease) caused by a deficiency in the debranching enzyme, leading to impaired glycogenolysis , glycogen accumulation in the liver, and fasting hypoglycemia.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following statements about mitochondria is NOT true?
options: Glycolytic enzymes are in mitochondrial matrix
Inner mitochondrial membrane contains enzymes of electron transport chain
Fluid matrix contains enzymes of TCA cycle
Acyl-CoA synthetase in localized in the outer mitochondrial membrane

explanation.short: Ans. A) Glycolytic enzymes are in mitochondrial matrix
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option B: The inner mitochondrial membrane contains enzymes of the electron transport chain: This is true . The inner mitochondrial membrane is the site of the electron transport chain , where ATP is synthesized.
• Option C : The fluid matrix contains enzymes of the TCA cycle: This is also true . The mitochondrial matrix houses enzymes of the TCA cycle, which is involved in energy production.
• Option D: Acyl-CoA synthetase is localized in the outer mitochondrial membrane: True, acyl-CoA synthetase , involved in fatty acid metabolism, is found on the outer mitochondrial membrane.
• Educational Objective:
• LOCATION OF ENZYMES IN MITOCHONDRIA

subject: Biochemistry
question:
The diagram below represents the various fates of pyruvate. Which letter on the diagram shows the reaction requiring biotin as a cofactor and is activated by acetylcoenzyme A?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/03/picture9.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. B: Denotes PDH present in mitochondria, which oxidatively decarboxylates pyruvate to acetyl CoA.
• Option C. D: Denotes ALT. Alanine is formed by transamination of pyruvate by the enzyme alanine aminotransferase (ALT), which requires PLP.
• Pyruvate + Glutamate → Alanine + alpha ketoglutarate
• Option D. A: Pyruvate kinase dephosphorylates phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvate, which is a substrate level key glycolytic enzyme.
• Educational Objective:
• The role of biotin is a cofactor in the carboxylation reaction catalyzed by pyruvate carboxylase and how acetyl-CoA serves as an allosteric activator of the enzyme, linking the state of cellular energy to gluconeogenesis.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A medical student is studying the unique metabolic pathways of erythrocytes (red blood cells). Due to the absence of mitochondria, erythrocytes rely on glycolysis for ATP production. Within this context, the student is interested in the yield of ATP from glucose through the Rapoport-Luebering cycle. What is the net gain of ATP by this cycle in red blood cells from one molecule of glucose?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objectives:
• Understand RBC Metabolism: Recognize that red blood cells lack mitochondria and rely exclusively on anaerobic glycolysis. Learn the Rapoport-Luebering Cycle: Know that this shunt allows RBCs to produce 2,3-BPG, regulating oxygen release from hemoglobin but at the cost of losing ATP production.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 6-year-old boy presents with hemolytic anemia, and further investigation reveals a deficiency in hexokinase, a key enzyme in the glycolytic pathway, leading to the premature destruction of energy-depleted red blood cells (RBCs). Which of the following statements about hexokinase is incorrect?
options: Has higher affinity to glucose compared to glucokinase
Has higher Km to glucose compared to glucokinase
Involved in providing glucose to the peripheral tissues
Not involved in glucose storage by the liver

explanation.short: Ans. B) Has higher Km to glucose compared to Glucokinase
explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A: Has higher affinity to glucose compared to Glucokinase.
• Option C: Involved in providing glucose to the peripheral tissues.
• Option D: Not involved in glucose storage by the liver
• Educational Objective:
• Glucose to glucose-6-phosphate is converted by either Glucokinase or hexokinase

subject: Biochemistry
question: A pediatric team is evaluating a 6-month-old full-term baby girl who presents with failure to thrive, hypotonia, decreased muscle mass, head lag, and metabolic acidosis. Her blood work shows elevated levels of lactate, pyruvate, and alanine. These clinical findings suggest a deficiency in a key metabolic enzyme. Which enzyme is most likely to be deficient in this infant, given her clinical and laboratory presentation?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A. Alanine aminotransferase: This enzyme is involved in the transamination of alanine to pyruvate . A deficiency would not likely result in elevated levels of pyruvate or lactate.
• Option B. Lactate dehydrogenase: A deficiency in this enzyme would typically lead to lower levels of lactate , not higher.
• Option C. Pyruvate carboxylase: While this enzyme is involved in gluconeogenesis , its deficiency usually leads to hypoglycemia and does not typically cause elevated lactate levels.
• Educational Objective:
• Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency is characterized by the accumulation of pyruvate that cannot be effectively converted into acetyl CoA, leading to its reduction to lactate resulting in lactic acidosis and transamination to alanine

subject: Biochemistry
question: In a fitness center, a personal trainer discusses with a client the metabolic changes that occur during intense aerobic workouts. The trainer explains how the body metabolizes glucose to meet the increased energy demands of vigorous exercise. The conversation leads to a question about the form in which carbon atoms from glucose enter the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle during such high-energy activities. What is the correct form in which these carbons enter the TCA cycle?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Citrate: Oxaloacetate condenses with acetyl CoA entering TCA cycle to form citrate (a tricarboxylic molecule ) (inside TCA cycle ) with the help of the enzyme citrate synthase.
• Option B. Oxaloacetate: Pyruvate is carboxylated to oxaloacetate which is then used for gluconeogenesis.
• Option D. Pyruvate: Glucose is converted to pyruvate which is then converted to acetyl-CoA → enter TCA cycle and Pyruvate as such cannot enter TCA cycle.
• Educational Objective:
• In aerobic glycolysis , glucose is converted into pyruvate → then c onverted to Acetyl-CoA , which actually enters the citric acid cycle. Two Acetyl-CoA , each containing two of the glucose’s carbons , are formed from each glucose molecule.

subject: Biochemistry
question:
Examines the following diagram, illustrating the PDH complex and its enzymatic components, labelled A, B, and C What is the name of enzyme component 'C' within the PDH complex?

[Image: https://cerebellum-web-static.s3.amazonaws.com/media/public/images/2024/04/02/picture21_2waVOtU.jpg]

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• Option A. Pyruvate dehydrogenase: This is the first enzyme in the complex and is responsible for the decarboxylation of pyruvate.
• Option B. Dihydrolipoyl transacetylase: The second enzyme in the complex, responsible for transferring the acetyl group to CoA to form acetyl-CoA.
• Option C. Lipoamide: Not an enzyme but rather a cofactor bound to the enzymes in the PDH complex.
• Educational Objective:
• The enzyme part of the PDH complex is made up of three component enzymes. PYRUVATE DEHYDROGENASE (ENZYME 1): Catalyses oxidative decarboxylation; Coenzyme: TPP; Enzyme bound hydroxyethyl TPP is formed DIHYDRO LIPOYL TRANSACETYLASE (ENZYME 2): Hydroxyethyl group is oxidized to form acetyl group → transferred from TPP to lipoamide → acetyl lipoamide. DIHYDROLIPOYL DEHYDROGENASE (ENZYME 3): Oxidation of lipoamide; cofactors: TPP, lipoamide and FAD are regenerated

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following statements about the oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetyl CoA is incorrect?
options: PDH complex is analogous to α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase
Fat can be converted to glucose, because PDH cycle is reversible
TPP, lipoic acid, coenzyme A, FAD, NAD are the coenzymes involved
PDH defect leads to lactic acidosis

explanation.short: Ans. B) Fat can be converted to glucose, because PDH cycle is reversible
explanation.detail:
• Explanation
• Option A: True , the PDH complex is analogous to the α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex in terms of function and coenzyme requirements.
• Option C: True , these coenzymes are all involved in the function of the PDH complex.
• Option D: True, a defect in the PDH complex can lead to lactic acidosis because pyruvate is then preferentially converted to lactate by lactate dehydrogenase .
• Educational Objective:
• The conversion of pyruvate to acetyl CoA via the PDH complex is an irreversible step in metabolism. This means that fat, which is broken down into acetyl CoA, cannot be converted back to glucose through this pathway. This is a fundamental concept in metabolism, as it explains why fatty acids cannot undergo gluconeogenesis to form glucose.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A neurology team is managing a 2-month-old boy with neurological symptoms and lactic acidosis. To determine the underlying metabolic issue, they perform an enzyme assay on extracts from the patient's cultured skin fibroblasts, assessing the activity of the pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) complex. They find that the complex operates at only 5% of the normal activity with a standard amount of thiamine pyrophosphate (TPP) but achieves 80% of normal activity with a much higher TPP concentration. What is the correct interpretation concerning the patient's condition and potential interventions?
options: Administration of thiamine is expected to reduce his serum lactate level and improve his clinical symptoms.
A high carbohydrate diet would be expected to be beneficial for this patient.
Citrate production from aerobic glycolysis is expected to be increased.
PDH kinase, a regulatory enzyme of the PDH complex, is expected to be active.

explanation.short: Ans. A) Administration of thiamine is expected to reduce his serum lactate level and improve his clinical symptoms
explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
• Thiamine (Vitamin B1) is the precursor for TPP , a coenzyme for PDH complex, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and transketolase . Some PDH deficiencies are thiamine-responsive , especially those with a mutated E1 subunit that has a high Km for TPP . Thiamine therapy can significantly reduce serum lactate and improve neurological symptoms in responsive cases

subject: Biochemistry
question: A genetic counselor is discussing the potential impact of enzyme deficiencies on metabolic pathways with a couple awaiting the birth of their child. The counselor explains that certain genetic conditions can disrupt normal metabolic processes. She asks the couple to consider the statement: "A defect in the pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) complex can lead to lactic acidosis." Is this statement true or false?

explanation.detail:
• Explanation:
• A PDH defect can indeed lead to lactic acidosis. The PDH complex is responsible for converting pyruvate to acetyl CoA, a step that links glycolysis to the citric acid cycle . If PDH is deficient or defective , pyruvate cannot effectively enter the citric acid cycle and is instead converted to lactate by lactate dehydrogenase. This can lead to an accumulation of lactate in the blood, resulting in lactic acidosis.
• Educational Objective:
• Significance of PDH Complex
• Thiamine deficiency affect PDH and thereby the complete oxidation of Glucose PDH defect can lead to Lactic Acidosis due to excess of pyruvate and conversion to lactate instead of acetyl CoA Fat cannot be converted to glucose because of the irreversible nature of PDH

subject: Biochemistry
question: PG Junior Residents of Biochemistry department are discussing a complex case of metabolic disorder; a debate arises regarding the metabolic flexibility of pyruvate under various physiological conditions. A senior consultant poses a question to the team to clarify the understanding of pyruvate metabolism. Which of the following is a fate of pyruvate in human metabolism?

explanation.detail:
• Educational Objective:
